title: Tropical Flag Varieties
overall_summary: The paper introduces flag Dressians as tropical analogues of partial flag varieties and proves that their points are equivalently valuated flag matroids, coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions, and flags of projective tropical linear spaces. A central technical innovation is the use of projective tropical linear spaces, which include matroids with loops and are characterized by circuits, cocircuit spans, coloopless cells in dual complexes, and projective closures. The most relevant consequence for studying ordered collections of tropical linear spaces is Theorem 4.3.1: for valuated matroids ¬µ and ŒΩ, the inclusion trop(¬µ) ‚äÜ trop(ŒΩ) is equivalent to the valuated matroid quotient relation ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ. Thus chains of tropical linear spaces can be encoded algebraically by valuated flag matroids and polyhedrally by flag-matroidal subdivisions, giving a framework for any attempted order-reversing operation on a poset of tropical subspaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix integers 0 ‚â§ r ‚â§ n, write [n] = {1,...,n}, and let T = R ‚à™ {‚àû}. For ¬µ ‚àà T^{C([n],r)}, where C([n],r) denotes the r-subsets of [n], view ¬µ as a function on r-subsets and as a weight on the point configuration {e_I ‚àà R^[n] : ¬µ(I) ‚âÝ ‚àû}. The following are equivalent: (a) the projective class of ¬µ in P(T^{C([n],r)}) lies in the Dressian Dr(r;n), i.e. satisfies all tropical Grassmann-Pl√ºcker relations; (b) the finite support of ¬µ is the set of bases of a rank-r matroid and the finite values define a valuated matroid, meaning that for every two bases B,B‚Ä≤ and every i ‚àà B‚àíB‚Ä≤ there exists j ‚àà B‚Ä≤‚àíB such that ¬µ(B)+¬µ(B‚Ä≤) ‚â• ¬µ(B‚àíi+j)+¬µ(B‚Ä≤‚àíj+i); (c) the coherent subdivision ‚àÜ_¬µ induced by the weight ¬µ on Conv(e_I : ¬µ(I) ‚âÝ ‚àû) has every face equal to the base polytope of a matroid. Consequently, for a fixed matroid M, the stratum Dr(M) = Dr(r;n) ‚à© T_{B(M)} parametrizes valuated matroids with underlying matroid M, equivalently weights on Q(M) inducing matroidal subdivisions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Extend ¬µ to a function Z^[n] ‚Üí T by putting ¬µ(e_I)=¬µ(I) on r-subset indicator vectors and ‚àû elsewhere. The tropical Grassmann-Pl√ºcker relations are exactly the M-convex exchange inequalities for this function, and these are exactly the valuated basis exchange axioms. M-convex functions have tight coherent subdivisions whose cells are generalized permutohedra, and conversely such subdivisions give M-convex functions. Since the domain lies in the hypersimplex, the Gelfand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova characterization says that generalized permutohedra contained in the hypersimplex are precisely matroid base polytopes. This gives the equivalence between tropical Pl√ºcker points, valuated matroids, and matroidal subdivisions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ : Z^[n] ‚Üí T be an M-convex function with finite effective domain Q = dom(¬µ), viewed as a weighted lattice point configuration in R^[n]. For a nonempty subset S ‚äÜ [n], let Q_{e_{[n]‚àíS}} be the face of Q minimizing the linear functional e_{[n]‚àíS}; let Q|S be its projection to R^S and Q/S its projection to R^{[n]‚àíS}. Then there exist weights ¬µ|S on Q|S and ¬µ/S on Q/S, each unique up to adding a global constant, such that the restriction of ¬µ to the face Q_{e_{[n]‚àíS}} is the product weight ¬µ|S √ó ¬µ/S, i.e. ¬µ(p,q)=¬µ|S(p)+¬µ/S(q) under Q_{e_{[n]‚àíS}} = Q|S √ó Q/S. Moreover ¬µ restricted to this face, ¬µ|S, and ¬µ/S are all M-convex functions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The effective domain of an M-convex function is a generalized permutohedron. The Hopf-monoid face factorization for generalized permutohedra gives Q_{e_{[n]‚àíS}} = Q|S √ó Q/S. To prove that the weight splits additively, it suffices to show that for p,p‚Ä≤ ‚àà Q|S and q,q‚Ä≤ ‚àà Q/S one has ¬µ(p,q)‚àí¬µ(p‚Ä≤,q)=¬µ(p,q‚Ä≤)‚àí¬µ(p‚Ä≤,q‚Ä≤). Because generalized permutohedra have edges parallel to e_i‚àíe_j, one reduces to p‚àíp‚Ä≤=e_i‚àíe_i‚Ä≤ with i,i‚Ä≤ ‚àà S and q‚àíq‚Ä≤=e_j‚àíe_j‚Ä≤ with j,j‚Ä≤ ‚àà [n]‚àíS. Applying the M-convex exchange axiom twice, once to ((p,q),(p‚Ä≤,q‚Ä≤)) and once to ((p,q‚Ä≤),(p‚Ä≤,q)), forces the desired equality. M-convexity of the face and factors follows from the characterization of M-convex functions by subdivisions into generalized permutohedra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3.8 and Corollary 2.3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ be an M-convex function on Z^[n], viewed as a weighted point configuration, and let Œ£_¬µ be the dual complex of its coherent subdivision in R^[n]/R1. For each cell œÉ of Œ£_¬µ, let closure(œÉ) denote its closure inside tropical projective space P(T^[n]). If ‚àÖ ‚âÝ S ‚äÜ [n] and T_S is the stratum of P(T^[n]) consisting of points with finite support exactly S, identified with R^S/R1, then {closure(œÉ) ‚à© T_S : œÉ ‚àà Œ£_¬µ} is exactly the dual complex Œ£_{¬µ|S}. Equivalently, for u ‚àà T_S written as u‚Ä≤ √ó ‚àû^{[n]‚àíS}, define ‚àÜ^u_¬µ := ‚àÜ^{u‚Ä≤}_{¬µ|S}; then P(T^[n]) is partitioned by the relative interiors {u : ‚àÜ^u_¬µ = F}, as F ranges over all faces of all subdivisions ‚àÜ_{¬µ|S} for nonempty S ‚äÜ [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A general boundary lemma for weighted point configurations says that near a boundary stratum T_S, the lower face selected by a nearby point in the dense torus depends first on minimizing e_{[n]‚àíS}; thus locally the dual complex is the dual complex of the restriction of the weight to the face A_{e_{[n]‚àíS}}. For M-convex functions, Lemma 2.3.7 splits that restricted weighted face as ¬µ|S √ó ¬µ/S. When one intersects the corresponding local product dual complex with the stratum T_S, only the R^S/R1 factor remains, producing Œ£_{¬µ|S}. This gives both the description of closures of cells and the projective extension of the face-cell correspondence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ be a valuated matroid of rank r on [n], and let S ‚äÜ [n] be nonempty. Valuated circuits are the non-all-‚àû vectors C_¬µ(A) ‚àà T^[n] for A ‚àà C([n],r+1), with coordinate C_¬µ(A)_i = ¬µ(A‚àíi) if i ‚àà A and ‚àû otherwise. Then the valuated circuits of the restriction ¬µ|S are exactly those valuated circuits C of ¬µ whose support is contained in S. The valuated circuits of the contraction ¬µ/S are the minimal-support elements among the projected vectors (C_i)_{i‚àà[n]‚àíS} as C ranges over valuated circuits of ¬µ. Moreover restriction and contraction are dual: (¬µ/S)^* = ¬µ^*|([n]‚àíS).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Baker-Bowler results on matroids over partial hyperstructures for this statement. The restriction formula keeps precisely those circuit vectors already supported in S. The contraction formula projects all circuits away from S and discards projected vectors whose support properly contains another projected circuit support, matching the usual minimal-support characterization of contracted circuits. Duality follows from the standard dual relation between deletion/restriction and contraction, applied to valuated matroids.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem B
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ be a valuated matroid on ground set [n], let M be its underlying matroid, and let ‚Ñì ‚äÜ [n] be the set of loops of M. In tropical projective space P(T^[n]), the following five subsets coincide. (i) The projective tropical linear space trop(¬µ) defined by trop(¬µ)=‚ãÉ_{‚àÖ‚äÜS‚ää[n]} (trop(¬µ/S) √ó {‚àû}^S), where ¬µ/S is the contraction to [n]‚àíS and the usual tropical linear space is used on the dense torus. (ii) The projective tropical prevariety cut out by all valuated circuits C of ¬µ: the set of u ‚àà P(T^[n]) such that the minimum among {C_i+u_i : i‚àà[n]} is attained at least twice, with the paper's convention for all-‚àû values. (iii) The union of coloopless cells in the projective closure of the dual complex of the dual valuated matroid ¬µ^*: the set of u ‚àà P(T^[n]) such that ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ^*} is the base polytope of a coloopless matroid. (iv) The tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of ¬µ, i.e. projective classes of finite tropical linear combinations (a_1‚äôC_1^*)‚äï...‚äï(a_m‚äôC_m^*) with a_i ‚àà R and C_i^* valuated cocircuits of ¬µ. (v) The closure in P(T^[n]) of trop(¬µ/‚Ñì) √ó {‚àû}^‚Ñì.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality between the circuit-prevariety description and the cocircuit-span description is cited from Murota-Tamura. The equality of (i) and (iii) follows by applying the usual dense-torus loopless-cell description to each contraction ¬µ/S, using (¬µ/S)^* = ¬µ^*|([n]‚àíS), and then using Theorem 2.3.8 to identify boundary strata of the projective closure of the dual complex. For (iii) ‚äÜ (v), after reducing to the loopless case, take a boundary point u √ó ‚àû^{[n]‚àíS} in a coloopless cell. Choose u‚Ä≤ in trop(¬µ|([n]‚àíS)) with positive coordinates. For large c, the nearby dense-torus points u √ó c u‚Ä≤ have selected dual faces that split as ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ^*|S} √ó ‚àÜ^{cu‚Ä≤}_{¬µ^*/S}; both factors are coloopless, so these nearby points lie in trop(¬µ), and the boundary point is in the closure. For (v) ‚äÜ (ii), circuit prevarieties are closed and agree with the usual tropical linear space on the dense torus; if loops are present, singleton loop circuits force the corresponding coordinates to be ‚àû. For (ii) ‚äÜ (i), restrict the circuit equations to a stratum T_{[n]‚àíS}; Theorem 3.1.6 identifies the resulting equations with the circuit equations for the contraction ¬µ/S, so Proposition 3.2.1 puts the point in trop(¬µ/S) √ó {‚àû}^S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.1.1 with Brylawski quotient criteria
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be matroids on the same finite ground set [n]. The paper writes M ‚Üû N and says that M is a matroid quotient of N if any, equivalently all, of the following conditions hold: (1) for all A ‚äÜ B ‚äÜ [n], rk_M(B)‚àírk_M(A) ‚â§ rk_N(B)‚àírk_N(A); (2) every circuit of N is a union of circuits of M; (3) there exists a matroid MÃÉ on [n] disjoint union [n‚Ä≤] such that M = MÃÉ/[n‚Ä≤] and N = MÃÉ delete [n‚Ä≤]; (4) N^* is a quotient of M^*. A sequence M = (M_1,...,M_k) of matroids on [n] is a flag matroid if M_i ‚Üû M_j for all i < j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of the four quotient criteria is cited from Brylawski. The paper uses the rank-function criterion to check minor stability of flag matroids, the circuit criterion to compare with tropical circuit equations, the extension criterion for rank-difference-one constructions, and the dual criterion to reverse quotient order under matroid duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r_1,...,r_k be nonnegative integers and let Q ‚äÇ R^[n] be a lattice polytope. First, Q is the base polytope of a flag matroid of rank sequence (r_1,...,r_k) if and only if Q is a generalized permutohedron and every vertex of Q is in the S_n-orbit of e_{1,...,r_1}+...+e_{1,...,r_k}. Second, if M=(M_1,...,M_k) is a flag matroid on [n] and S ‚äÜ [n], then M|S=(M_1|S,...,M_k|S) and M/S=(M_1/S,...,M_k/S) are flag matroids, and the face Q(M)_{e_{[n]‚àíS}} is the product Q(M|S) √ó Q(M/S).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The polytope characterization is cited from Borovik-Gelfand-White. For the minor statement, the fact that restrictions and contractions preserve the flag quotient relations follows directly from the rank-function characterization of matroid quotients. The product decomposition of the face is obtained by applying the generalized-permutohedron face factorization to the Minkowski-sum base polytope Q(M)=Q(M_1)+...+Q(M_k), together with the fact that faces of Minkowski sums are sums of the corresponding faces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0 ‚â§ r ‚â§ s ‚â§ n. Let ¬µ ‚àà T^{C([n],r)} and ŒΩ ‚àà T^{C([n],s)}. The image of ¬µ √ó ŒΩ in P(T^{C([n],r)}) √ó P(T^{C([n],s)}) lies in the flag Dressian FlDr(r,s;n), defined by the tropical Grassmann-Pl√ºcker relations for ranks r and s together with the tropical incidence-Pl√ºcker relations, if and only if ¬µ and ŒΩ are valuated matroids and ¬µ is a valuated matroid quotient of ŒΩ. Here ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ means that, for every basis I of the underlying matroid of ¬µ, every basis J of the underlying matroid of ŒΩ, and every i ‚àà I‚àíJ, there exists j ‚àà J‚àíI such that ¬µ(I)+ŒΩ(J) ‚â• ¬µ(I‚àíi+j)+ŒΩ(J‚àíj+i). More generally, points of FlDr(r_1,...,r_k;n) correspond to valuated flag matroids of rank sequence (r_1,...,r_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The tropical Grassmann-Pl√ºcker equations separately force ¬µ and ŒΩ to be valuated matroids by Theorem 3.1.3. For the incidence equations, take I‚Ä≤ ‚àà C([n],r‚àí1) and J‚Ä≤ ‚àà C([n],s+1). Choosing i ‚àà J‚Ä≤‚àíI‚Ä≤ and setting I=I‚Ä≤‚à™i and J=J‚Ä≤‚àíi rewrites the corresponding tropical incidence polynomial as P_I‚äôP_J ‚äï ‚äï_{j‚ààJ‚àíI} P_{I‚àíi+j}‚äôP_{J‚àíj+i}. The condition that its minimum be attained at least twice is precisely the assertion that the term ¬µ(I)+ŒΩ(J) is not the unique minimum, equivalently that some j gives the valuated quotient inequality. Running through all I‚Ä≤,J‚Ä≤ is the same as running through all I,J,i in the quotient axiom.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ and ŒΩ be valuated matroids of ranks r and s on a common ground set [n]. Then ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ as valuated matroids if and only if the associated projective tropical linear spaces satisfy trop(¬µ) ‚äÜ trop(ŒΩ) inside P(T^[n]). Consequently, a sequence ¬µ=(¬µ_1,...,¬µ_k) of valuated matroids is a valuated flag matroid if and only if trop(¬µ_1) ‚äÜ ... ‚äÜ trop(¬µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem B(iv), trop(¬µ) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits C^*(¬µ). Hence trop(¬µ) ‚äÜ trop(ŒΩ) is equivalent to requiring every valuated cocircuit of ¬µ to lie in trop(ŒΩ). By Theorem B(ii), trop(ŒΩ) is cut out by the tropical hyperplanes associated to valuated circuits of ŒΩ. A valuated cocircuit of ¬µ indexed by I‚Ä≤ ‚àà C([n],r‚àí1) and a valuated circuit of ŒΩ indexed by J‚Ä≤ ‚àà C([n],s+1) give the condition that the minimum of {ŒΩ(J‚Ä≤‚àíj‚Ä≤)+¬µ(I‚Ä≤‚à™j‚Ä≤) : j‚Ä≤‚ààJ‚Ä≤‚àíI‚Ä≤} is attained at least twice. These are exactly the tropical incidence-Pl√ºcker relations. Proposition 4.2.3 identifies those relations with the valuated quotient condition ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ, ŒΩ, and Œæ be valuated matroids on [n]. The following hold. (1) Valuated matroid quotients compose: if ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ and ŒΩ ‚Üû Œæ, then ¬µ ‚Üû Œæ. (2) Quotients are preserved by minors: if ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ and S ‚äÜ [n], then ¬µ/S ‚Üû ŒΩ/S and the deletions of S from ¬µ and ŒΩ also form a quotient. (3) For every S ‚äÇ [n], a valuated matroid satisfies ¬µ/S ‚Üû deletion_S(¬µ). (4) If ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ, then the underlying ordinary matroids M and N satisfy M ‚Üû N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), translate quotients into inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces using Theorem 4.3.1 and compose inclusions. For (2), the contraction statement follows from the stratum-by-stratum definition of projective tropical linear spaces in Theorem B(i); the deletion statement follows by duality because ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ is equivalent to ŒΩ^* ‚Üû ¬µ^*. For (3), Theorem 3.1.6 gives an inclusion between the relevant cocircuit sets after deletion and contraction, and Theorem B(iv) plus Theorem 4.3.1 converts this into a quotient. For (4), Theorem B(iv) implies that every cocircuit support of M is a union of cocircuit supports of N; equivalently N^* ‚Üû M^*, hence M ‚Üû N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem A
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ=(¬µ_1,...,¬µ_k) be a sequence of valuated matroids on [n], with underlying matroids M=(M_1,...,M_k), and assume M is a flag matroid. Let B(M)=B(M_1)+...+B(M_k) be the Minkowski-sum base configuration and Q(M) its convex hull. Then the following are equivalent: (a) ¬µ is a point of the flag Dressian FlDr(M), i.e. it satisfies the tropical incidence-Pl√ºcker relations in addition to the individual tropical Grassmann-Pl√ºcker relations; (b) ¬µ is a valuated flag matroid with underlying flag matroid M, meaning ¬µ_i ‚Üû ¬µ_j for all i<j; (c) the Minkowski-sum weight ¬µ_1+...+¬µ_k induces a coherent subdivision of Q(M) into base polytopes of flag matroids; (d) the projective tropical linear spaces form a flag trop(¬µ_1) ‚äÜ ... ‚äÜ trop(¬µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence (a) ‚áî (b) is Proposition 4.2.3 applied to all pairs of ranks. The equivalence (b) ‚áî (d) is Theorem 4.3.1 applied pairwise, using transitivity of inclusion and of valuated quotients. The implication (b) ‚áí (c) is Theorem 4.4.2: faces of the Minkowski-sum subdivision are sums of initial matroids, and initial valuated quotients have underlying ordinary quotient matroids. The implication (c) ‚áí (d) is Theorem 4.4.3: from a flag-matroidal subdivision one recovers valuated matroids and uses the coloopless-cell characterization of projective tropical linear spaces to prove the inclusions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ¬µ=(¬µ_1,...,¬µ_k) be a valuated flag matroid with underlying flag matroid M=(M_1,...,M_k) on [n]. Regard each ¬µ_i as a weight on the base configuration B(M_i). Then the Minkowski-sum weight ¬µ_1+...+¬µ_k on B(M)=B(M_1)+...+B(M_k) induces a flag-matroidal subdivision of B(M), hence a subdivision of Q(M) whose cells are base polytopes of flag matroids.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any u ‚àà R^[n]/R1, the face selected by u in the subdivision of the Minkowski sum is ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ_1}+...+‚àÜ^u_{¬µ_k}. Adding the same linear functional to each ¬µ_i does not change the valuated quotient relations and lets one reduce to u=0. The minimum face of a valuated matroid is the underlying matroid of its initial valuated matroid. If ¬µ_i ‚Üû ¬µ_j, then taking minima preserves the valuated quotient relation, and Corollary 4.3.2(4) says the underlying initial matroids form an ordinary matroid quotient. Thus every face is the base configuration of a flag matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4.4 and Theorem 4.4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 4.4.4: If M=(M_1,...,M_k) is a flag matroid and M_i is loopless for some i, then M_j is loopless for every j>i; equivalently, if M_i^* is coloopless, then M_j^* is coloopless for every j>i. Theorem 4.4.3: Let M=(M_1,...,M_k) be a flag matroid on [n], let w be a weight on the base configuration B(M), and suppose the coherent subdivision ‚àÜ_w is flag-matroidal. If ¬µ_1,...,¬µ_k are weights on B(M_1),...,B(M_k) such that ‚àÜ_w = ‚àÜ_{¬µ_1+...+¬µ_k}, then each ¬µ_i is a valuated matroid and trop(¬µ_1) ‚äÜ ... ‚äÜ trop(¬µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.4.4, the rank-function criterion for matroid quotients implies that if an element has rank zero in M_j, then it has rank zero in all earlier quotient matroids M_i; dualizing gives the coloop statement in the direction needed later. For Theorem 4.4.3, since every face of ‚àÜ_w is a Minkowski sum of matroid base polytopes, each factor face ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ_i} is a matroid base polytope; Theorem 3.1.3 therefore makes each ¬µ_i a valuated matroid. Passing to dual valuated matroids sends Q(M_i) to ‚àíQ(M_i)+1, so the dual weights induce a flag-matroidal subdivision for the reversed dual flag. For a boundary point u ‚àà P(T^[n]) in stratum T_S, Lemmas 2.3.4 and 2.3.7 identify the selected face ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ_i^*} with the appropriate restriction factor of a face in that dual flag subdivision. Hence the sequence of selected dual faces is a dual flag matroid. Lemma 4.4.4 then implies that colooplessness propagates from i to all j>i. By Theorem B(iii), colooplessness of ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ_i^*} is exactly the condition u ‚àà trop(¬µ_i), so u ‚àà trop(¬µ_i) implies u ‚àà trop(¬µ_j) for j>i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2.9 and Corollary 4.4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 2.2.9: Let A=A_1+...+A_k be a Minkowski sum of integral homogeneous point configurations, with the simplifying assumption that if dim Conv(A_i)=1 then |A_i|=2. If a weight w on A induces a coherent subdivision ‚àÜ_w that is mixed, then there exist weights w_i on A_i such that ‚àÜ_{w_1+...+w_k}=‚àÜ_w. Corollary 4.4.5: Every coherent flag-matroidal subdivision of the base polytope of a flag matroid arises from a valuated flag matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Theorem 2.2.9, write each mixed cell over u as F_{1,u}+...+F_{k,u}. For each factor i, partition R^E/R1 by the condition F_{i,u}=F_{i,u‚Ä≤}; the closures form a polyhedral complex Œ£_i coarsening the dual complex of ‚àÜ_w. At any nonmaximal cell, the star of Œ£_i is the normal fan of the lattice polytope Conv(F_{i,u}), so the union of codimension-one cells is rational, pure, and balanced. A standard tropical convexity result then realizes Œ£_i as the dual complex of some weighted point configuration. Using connectedness of maximal cells through codimension one and the one-dimensional assumption, the necessary dilation between this auxiliary configuration and the actual vertices of A_i is global; this assigns weights w_i with the prescribed factor faces. Lemma 2.2.6 then gives ‚àÜ_{Œ£w_i}=‚àÜ_w. For Corollary 4.4.5, a flag-matroidal subdivision is mixed; Theorem 2.2.9 supplies Minkowski-summand weights, and Theorem 4.4.3 shows these weights form a valuated flag matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1.2 and Corollary 5.1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [n~]={0,1,...,n}. Consider the partially defined coordinate-splitting map P(T^{C([n~],r+1)}) ‚á¢ P(T^{C([n],r)}) √ó P(T^{C([n],r+1)}) sending a valuated rank-(r+1) object on [n~] to the coordinates containing 0, with 0 removed, and the coordinates not containing 0. Let Œ©(r+1;[n~]) be the locus where both coordinate blocks are not all ‚àû. Then the induced map Œ©(r+1;[n~]) ‚à© Dr(r+1;n+1) ‚Üí FlDr(r,r+1;n) is surjective. The fiber over a point (¬µ‚Ä≤,¬µ), where ¬µ‚Ä≤ has rank r and ¬µ has rank r+1 on [n], is {a‚äô¬µ‚Ä≤ ‚äï b‚äô¬µ : a,b ‚àà R} ‚âÖ R^2/R(1,1), where ¬µ‚Ä≤ is embedded on bases containing 0 and ¬µ on bases not containing 0. Consequently, under the identification T^{C([n+1],r+1)} ‚âÖ T^{C([n],r)} √ó T^{C([n],r+1)}, the affine cones over Dr(r+1;n+1) and FlDr(r,r+1;n) are identical.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Well-definedness follows from Corollary 4.3.2(3): deleting and contracting an element of a valuated matroid give a valuated quotient. For surjectivity, begin with a valuated quotient ¬µ‚Ä≤ ‚Üû ¬µ of adjacent ranks and let M‚Ä≤ ‚Üû M be its underlying quotient. The classical adjacent-rank quotient construction gives a unique matroid MÃÉ on {0,...,n} with bases {I‚Ä≤‚à™0 : I‚Ä≤‚ààB(M‚Ä≤)} ‚à™ B(M), satisfying M‚Ä≤=MÃÉ/0 and M=MÃÉ delete 0. Define a weight ¬µÃÉ by a+¬µ‚Ä≤ on bases containing 0 and b+¬µ on bases not containing 0. The base polytope Q(MÃÉ) is the Cayley polytope of Q(M‚Ä≤) and Q(M); by the Cayley trick, cells of the subdivision induced by ¬µÃÉ correspond to cells of the subdivision induced by (a+¬µ‚Ä≤)+(b+¬µ) on B(M‚Ä≤)+B(M). Theorem 4.4.2 makes those cells flag-matroidal, and the adjacent-rank matroid construction turns them into matroid base polytopes, so Theorem 3.1.3 shows ¬µÃÉ is a valuated matroid. The fiber description records the only freedom: independent additive constants on the two coordinate blocks, modulo global projective scaling. The affine-cone statement also checks the cases where one block is all ‚àû, corresponding to 0 being a loop or coloop.

proof_techniques.name: Encode inclusion by valuated matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper turns the geometric condition trop(¬µ) ‚äÜ trop(ŒΩ) into the algebraic condition ¬µ ‚Üû ŒΩ. The proof uses two dual descriptions of a projective tropical linear space: it is the tropical span of valuated cocircuits, and it is cut out by valuated circuit tropical hyperplanes. Testing each cocircuit of ¬µ against each circuit of ŒΩ produces exactly the tropical incidence-Pl√ºcker relations, which are equivalent to the valuated quotient exchange inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A poset of tropical linear subspaces can be studied via quotient relations among valuated matroids. To construct or rule out an order-reversing map, one can try to define it on the corresponding valuated quotient data and check that it reverses the quotient relation.

proof_techniques.name: Use projective tropical linear spaces to handle loops and boundary strata
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces ordinary tropical linear spaces in R^[n]/R1 by projective tropical linear spaces in P(T^[n]). This is essential because restrictions, contractions, and flags naturally create loops; ordinary tropical linear spaces of matroids with loops are empty, while projective tropical linear spaces retain the correct boundary pieces. The projective space is decomposed into strata T_S according to finite support, and the intersection with T_{[n]‚àíS} is the tropical linear space of the contraction ¬µ/S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The flat-indexed subspaces in the prompt involve contractions together with added loops. The projective formalism is the right language for comparing such objects without losing information at the boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Coloopless-cell criterion in the dual complex
proof_techniques.description: Theorem B identifies trop(¬µ) with the set of projective points u for which the selected face ‚àÜ^u_{¬µ^*} in the dual valuated matroid subdivision is a coloopless matroid base polytope. This converts inclusion questions for tropical linear spaces into statements about propagation of colooplessness through families of matroidal faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution might be easier to study on dual subdivisions or face posets: one can attempt to show that a candidate map sends the coloopless-cell condition for one valuated matroid to the corresponding condition for the reversed object.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary control via M-convex face factorization
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex functions, faces toward a boundary stratum split as products ¬µ|S √ó ¬µ/S, and closures of dual-complex cells meet the stratum T_S in the dual complex of ¬µ|S. This gives precise control of how tropical linear spaces and their subdivisions degenerate along projective boundary strata.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of the lattice of flats into a Dressian-like poset is boundary/minor flavored. The factorization technique can be used to check whether a proposed involution is compatible with all flats by reducing to restrictions and contractions on strata.

proof_techniques.name: Flags as coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a valuated flag matroid ¬µ_1,...,¬µ_k by the Minkowski-sum weight ¬µ_1+...+¬µ_k on the base configuration B(M_1)+...+B(M_k). The cells of the induced subdivision are base polytopes of flag matroids, and conversely coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions arise from valuated flag matroids under the mixed-subdivision criterion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Chains in an inclusion poset of tropical linear spaces can be modeled as points of a flag Dressian or as flag-matroidal subdivisions. An anti-involution on the poset might be sought as a transformation of these coherent subdivisions that reverses the order of the Minkowski summands.

proof_techniques.name: Propagation of looplessness or colooplessness through a flag
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.4.4 shows that in a flag matroid M_1 ‚Üû ... ‚Üû M_k, looplessness propagates forward in rank, and dually colooplessness propagates forward for the corresponding dual sequence. This simple rank-function observation is the key local step in proving that flag-matroidal subdivisions yield inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Order reversal often exchanges loop-like and coloop-like behavior. This propagation lemma gives a concrete criterion to verify monotonicity or antimonotonicity of a candidate operation on chains.

proof_techniques.name: Adjacent-rank quotients via one-element extensions
proof_techniques.description: For a quotient of matroids or valuated matroids whose ranks differ by one, the paper encodes the pair as deletion and contraction of a single valuated matroid on one larger ground set. Tropically, this gives a surjective map from a subset of an ordinary Dressian Dr(r+1;n+1) to the adjacent-rank flag Dressian FlDr(r,r+1;n), with one-dimensional fibers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the poset problem can be reduced to covering relations or adjacent-dimensional inclusions, this construction lets one replace a two-level inclusion by a single valuated matroid with a distinguished element, where duality or a flat involution may be easier to express.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit-cocircuit testing of candidate maps
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 4.3.1 gives a practical test for inclusion: every valuated cocircuit of the smaller space must satisfy every valuated circuit equation of the larger space. This test is equivalent to all incidence-Pl√ºcker relations and avoids direct comparison of polyhedral supports.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed order-reversing involution Œ¶, one can attempt to prove L ‚äÜ L‚Ä≤ implies Œ¶(L‚Ä≤) ‚äÜ Œ¶(L) by transforming circuits of Œ¶(L) and cocircuits of Œ¶(L‚Ä≤) and checking the resulting tropical minima.

other_useful_info: Notation note: in this paper Dr(M) denotes the usual Dressian stratum parametrizing valuated matroids with fixed underlying matroid M, whereas the original problem uses relative Dressian for tropical linear subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space. The bridge supplied by the paper is not the notation but Theorem 4.3.1 and Theorem A, which turn inclusions and chains of projective tropical linear spaces into valuated quotient and flag-Dressian data. Remark 4.1.6 emphasizes that loops cannot be discarded in flag settings, motivating the projective definitions; this is directly relevant whenever contractions plus added loops appear. Remark 4.3.3 extends the quotient-inclusion dictionary to valuated matroid morphisms for a map of finite sets œÜ: [n]‚Üí[m], where ŒΩ ‚ÜÝ œÜ^{-1}¬µ is equivalent to a commutative diagram trop(¬µ) ‚Üí trop(ŒΩ) inside P(T^[m]) ‚Üí P(T^[n]). The paper also proves small-ground-set realizability of all valuated flag matroids for n‚â§5 and gives examples of nonrealizable valuated flag matroids on 6 elements, which may be useful for testing conjectural constructions but is not central to the order-reversal question.
why_relevant: Proves equivalences among valuated flag matroids, flags of projective tropical linear spaces, and coherent flag matroidal subdivisions. Since chains in Dr(M) are flags of tropical subspaces, this supplies the main flag-Dressian framework.
